seoTitle: Create KPI Cards in Power BI: The Basics
metaDescription: Learn the basics of creating KPI cards in Power BI, from your first card to customizing design and integrating multiple data sources.
intro: Cards are the simplest way to put a single important number front and center in Power BI, and mastering the basics gives you a building block you'll reuse in nearly every report. This guide covers creating your first card, customizing its design, and connecting it to the right data. It's aimed at beginners getting comfortable with core Power BI visuals.

steps.heading: Create your first card
steps.body: Add the Card visual to your report canvas and drag a measure or field into it to display that single value front and center.

steps.heading: Customize card design
steps.body: Adjust font size, color, and background so the card fits the visual style of the rest of your report while staying easy to read.

steps.heading: Integrate data sources
steps.body: Connect the card to measures built from your data model so it reflects live calculations rather than a static number.

steps.heading: Follow best practices for effective use
steps.body: Reserve cards for your most important top-line metrics, and pair them with supporting charts nearby for additional context.

faq.question: What's the difference between a Card and a KPI visual in Power BI?
faq.answer: A Card shows a single value with minimal context, while a KPI visual adds a trend line and comparison against a target alongside the current value.

faq.question: Can a card show text values instead of numbers?
faq.answer: Yes, cards can display text fields as well as numeric measures, though they're most commonly used for showing key numeric metrics.

faq.question: How do I make a card update automatically with filters?
faq.answer: As long as the card's value is based on a measure connected to your data model, it will automatically respond to slicers and filters applied elsewhere on the page.

faq.question: Is there a limit to how many cards I can add to one report page?
faq.answer: There's no hard limit, but too many cards on one page reduces their visual impact — a focused handful of key metrics reads better than a wall of numbers.
